text.title: ASP.NET Core: Entity Framework Core Patterns
text.summary: EF Core entity definition, IEntityTypeConfiguration, repository interface and implementation, AsNoTracking for reads, and paged queries.

rule.statement: Define all entity mappings in IEntityTypeConfiguration<T> classes, not via data annotations on the entity. Apply via modelBuilder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly() in DbContext.
rule.rationale: IEntityTypeConfiguration separates persistence concerns from the domain entity. Data annotations on entities couple the domain model to EF-specific attributes, preventing reuse in non-EF contexts.

rule.statement: Always call AsNoTracking() on read-only repository queries. Use AsSplitQuery() when a query includes multiple collection Includes to avoid Cartesian explosion.
rule.rationale: EF Core tracks every loaded entity by default, consuming memory proportional to result set size. Read-only queries with AsNoTracking() are ~30% faster and use less memory. Multiple Include()s on collections without AsSplitQuery() produce a JOIN with row multiplication.

rule.statement: Repository methods that return lists must use AsNoTracking() and expose a PagedResult<T> return type that includes TotalCount, Page, and PageSize. Never return IQueryable from a repository — the query must execute inside the repository.
rule.rationale: IQueryable leaking out of the repository breaks the repository abstraction: callers can add arbitrary LINQ operators that bypass intended query constraints. PagedResult encapsulates pagination metadata so callers do not duplicate count queries.

rule.statement: Call SaveChangesAsync() only in the repository (or a unit of work), not in service methods. The service orchestrates but the repository owns the persistence boundary.
rule.rationale: Services calling SaveChangesAsync() directly bypass the repository abstraction and make it impossible to batch multiple operations into a single unit of work without leaking EF context into the service.

text.reference: IEntityTypeConfiguration with indexes, FK cascade, many-to-many
